Colleague brief · ~8 min read · Jul 2026

China AI Models: The 5-Minute Version

Goldman’s China LLM primer (via ZeroHedge) is ~50 pages of bank prose. This is the shareable distillation: capability catch-up, the emerging three-way price fight, non-China enterprise adoption, and who can actually afford to win.

Source: GS (Ronald Keung) via ZeroHedge · UBS · xAI API pricing Focus: non-China evolution · price war · 2H26 Self-contained · offline-ready Produced by Gilles DEMANEUF with Grok 4.5
1. Bottom line 2. Pricing snapshot 3. What China figured out 4. Non-China evolution 5. Price fight trajectory 6. Winners & risks
Bottom line

Chinese open-source / open-weight models (Zhipu GLM-5.2, DeepSeek V4, Alibaba Qwen3.7 Max, MiniMax M3) have closed roughly ~90–95% of the capability gap to US frontier models while pricing at ~10–25% of US flagship cost. That is no longer a “DeepSeek moment” novelty — Goldman frames it as a structural efficiency lead (smaller MoE models, low activation ratios, aggressive inference engineering).

For 2H26 the more interesting story is not pure China-vs-US. It is an emerging three-way price war: China low-end/agentic undercutting + China top-tier coding at ~$1 blended, plus a US-side disruptor in Grok 4.5 at $2 / $6 that compresses the “US premium” Chinese models were arbitraging against.

1. Pricing snapshot — the three camps (Jul 2026)

API list prices; blended figures for China as cited by Goldman. US flagship numbers are approximate headline tiers.

Camp Model Approx. price Read-through
US flagship GPT-5.x top tier / Claude Opus-class ~$5 in / $25–30 out Still owns max-capability premium; under pressure from below
US disruptor Grok 4.5 (xAI) $2 / $6 Flagship claims (coding / agentic / low hallucination) at ~⅓–⅕ of OpenAI/Anthropic flagship price — “DeepSeek move” inside the US stack
US mid Claude Sonnet-class / flash tiers ~$2–3 / $10–15 Where enterprises park default workload as budgets bite
China top GLM-5.2, Qwen3.7 Max ~$1 blended ~5× China’s low end; still ~80% cheaper than US flagship; 10–20% GPM (GSe)
China low DeepSeek V4 Flash / agentic pack $0.06–0.35 blended Volume / SME / 24-7 agents; thin or negative margins, war-chest subsidized
US flagship (~blended)
~$8–12
Grok 4.5 (blended-ish)
~$3–4
China top coding
~$1
China agentic low-end
~$0.2

Bar widths are illustrative relative to a ~$8–12 US flagship blended reference — not a precise formula.

Colleague takeaway: Grok 4.5 (launched mid-July 2026) is the single biggest Western disruption in this setup. It forces OpenAI/Anthropic to defend flagship pricing against a same-ecosystem rival claiming superior coding/agentic performance at a fraction of the cost — and it shrinks the arbitrage window Chinese models had against pure US premiums.

2. What China figured out (and why it’s sticky)

1 Efficiency is the product, not a side effect. Chinese flagships run ~200bn–1.6T total parameters with only ~3–5% activated (MoE / sparse attention). That is why they can land near-frontier Arena/coding scores at $1 blended while US dense SOTA models sit at $4–8.
2 Two-tier market, not one race. Premium coding/intelligence models keep pricing power (~$1 China / multi-dollar US). The agentic / SME / “agents per day” tier is already in a price war at $0.06–0.2 — and Goldman expects that low end to stay fragmented.
3 Open source → open weight is the monetization path. Free MIT-style open source understates true deployment (hyperscalers host GLM etc. with no take rate). Expect more Community Licenses (MiniMax-style commercial terms) so inference gross profit can cover training.

3. Non-China evolution — what actually changed for Western buyers

Goldman’s global research angle is less “China invades the US” and more enterprises pivoting from token-maxxing to ROI-first — and that pivot is exactly the opening for cheap, good-enough models.

From “burn tokens” to “cost per task”

  • Token-maxxing broke budgets. Jellyfish study (cited by GS): heavy AI users burned ~10× tokens for only ~2× output. Multiple US internet cos (Apr 2026) reportedly blew a full-year agentic budget in ~4 months and imposed monthly caps / killed vanity agent leaderboards.
  • New metrics: Daily Active Agents (DAA), Agentic Work Units (AWU), and cost per completed task beat raw $/token as the management KPI.
  • Default model downgrade: SOTA reserved for high-value coding; flash / China / mid-tier for routine agent loops.

Where Chinese models show up outside China

  • Already on US hyperscalers: Google Model Garden / Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform and AWS Bedrock list DeepSeek, MiniMax, Moonshot, GLM, Qwen — fully managed inside US cloud estates (data residency argument).
  • Microsoft optionality: Nadella (WSJ) floated optional DeepSeek-class models inside Copilot/Azure so customers get a cheaper choice without leaving Azure.
  • SME / non-US markets: MiniMax reportedly ~60–70% revenue overseas; agentic Hermes/Claw-style 24/7 agents are the wedge product.
  • Global token demand backdrop: GS US team: agentic AI → ~24× token growth by 2030 (enterprise agents ~55×).
Geopolitics is the off-switch. Reuters (7 Jul 2026): Beijing may curb overseas access to China’s most advanced models. If enforced, the cheapest global frontier options shrink — ironically re-widening pricing power for US / Grok at the top tier. Western entity-list / market-access risk cuts the other way.

Infrastructure note

  • LongCat 2.0 (Meituan, Jun 30): 1.6T MoE, 1M context, trained end-to-end on ~50k domestic cards (Huawei Atlas-class). Signals derisking from Nvidia for training, not just inference.
  • Access to high-end leased training compute remains a GS “swing factor” for iteration pace.
  • China hyperscalers’ capex/OCF ratios still look financeable vs. US peers (see full note).

4. Likely price fight — 2H26 trajectory

Think in three layers, not one global race to $0:

LayerDynamicsWho bleeds / who wins
Frontier / flagship Grok 4.5 undercuts OpenAI/Anthropic on headline price while claiming coding/agentic parity-or-better. China top coding sits ~$1 if accessible overseas. Pressure on US list prices & enterprise discounting; winners differentiate on reliability, harness, data residency, SLAs — not pure Arena.
Default / mid-tier Enterprises multi-home: Sonnet/flash + Grok + hosted Qwen/GLM/DeepSeek. Cost-per-task shopping. Volume shifts to whoever is “good enough + integrated.” Hyperscaler model gardens win distribution.
Agentic low-end Already a knife fight at $0.06–0.2. Mega-caps can subsidize longer. Coding consolidates; agents stay fragmented (GS view). Gross margins thin/negative near-term; only balance sheets + inference efficiency survive a multi-year war.
GS 2H26 signposts: harness/agentic apps as entry points (ZCode, Workbuddy, Qoder); multi-model enterprise stacks; new China launches at 2–5T total params; possible shift open-source → open-weight on best models; multi-modal upgrades; potential export curbs on frontier China models.

Three messages to leave with the room

1 China’s edge is structural efficiency, not a temporary discount. MoE + low activation + inference tricks mean the cost floor is lower — and that floor now anchors global pricing conversations.
2 Bifurcation, not consolidation. Premium tier keeps some pricing power; agentic low-end is commoditizing. Grok just imported that bifurcation into the US flagship shelf.
3 Winners = ARR scale × gross margin × balance sheet. Benchmarks get you into the game. Cash and cost-to-serve decide who is still standing when the price fight runs into 2027.

5. Who’s positioned — and the risks that matter

Goldman’s competitive framework scores pricing power (time-to-market, Arena, realized price), cost advantage (throughput, cache hit rate, activation ratio, inference GPM), and financial strength (cash, access to compute). Overlay: token scale and share progression.

  • Text / foundation: Zhipu (Knowledge Atlas) and DeepSeek strongest on GS framing.
  • Multi-modal: ByteDance (Seedance / Seed stack).
  • Independents’ implied value: GS notes aggregate >$200bn across latest rounds / listings.
  • US side (our overlay): OpenAI / Anthropic capital vs. xAI war chest decides who can match a multi-year price fight without gutting R&D.

Key risks (keep these on the slide)

Downside / blockers
  • Beijing curbs overseas access to frontier models
  • Western market-access / entity-list actions
  • Training-compute constraints (export controls)
  • Open-source leakage → ARR under-capture
  • Prolonged low-end price war → delayed profitability
Upside / accelerants
  • ROI-first enterprise shift → share to value models
  • Hyperscaler distribution (Bedrock, Model Garden, maybe Copilot)
  • Domestic silicon path (LongCat-class training)
  • Open-weight take rates repair unit economics
  • Agentic token explosion (GS: 24× by 2030 globally)

One-line for the email subject

“China is ~95% as good at ~20% of the price — and Grok just started the same fight inside the US camp. Expect bifurcation + price pressure, not a single winner, through 2H26.”
